preFix.title: Addressing the ground-floor perimeter cold bridge
preFix.subtitle: Insulating the floor-to-wall junction in a solid-floor or suspended-floor property
preFix.excerpt: The junction between the ground-floor slab and the external wall is the most commonly missed cold bridge in UK retrofits. Even when both the floor and wall are insulated, leaving the perimeter strip unaddressed means heat continues to flow diagonally out through the corner. This is how to close it.
preFix.sourceNotes: gov.uk Building Regulations Approved Document L1B (2022): linear thermal transmittance (psi-value) thresholds for junctions. BRE Report BR 497 on thermal bridging in UK construction. AECB guidance on perimeter insulation continuity. Energy Saving Trust retrofit guidance for floor-wall junctions.

content.text: Insulating a solid concrete floor or a suspended timber floor addresses the main horizontal area. Adding internal wall insulation addresses the vertical surface. But neither alone closes what happens in the corner: the last 200-300 mm of floor slab and the bottom of the wall remain as an unbroken path for heat to travel diagonally from the warm interior to the cold outside. This corner is a
thermal bridge
. Building engineers call it the floor-to-wall perimeter junction, and its thermal performance is expressed as a linear thermal transmittance (psi-value, in W/m·K).

For a house with 30 m of external perimeter (a 1930s semi, roughly), a psi-value of 0.3 W/m·K for the floor-to-wall junction loses around 800 kWh per year at a 10°C average temperature difference. That is about 50 pounds at 2025/26 gas cap rates. Addressing it saves 400-600 kWh per year depending on how much of the junction is exposed and what material it passes through.
The three scenarios
The right approach depends on which insulation work has been done already and which is planned.
Floor insulation only (no wall insulation): adding a vertical strip of 50-75 mm
PIR board
to the base of the wall from floor to 200-300 mm up, bonded and sealed, reduces the psi-value from ~0.3 to ~0.1 W/m·K. This alone saves roughly 400 kWh per year on the 30 m perimeter example.

Wall insulation only (no floor insulation): run the internal wall insulation board down behind the skirting board and onto the top surface of the floor slab by 100-150 mm before fixing the skirting. The skirting hides the horizontal return.

Both floor and wall insulation planned: sequence the work so the floor insulation is installed first, the wall insulation runs down and laps over the floor insulation edge by 50 mm, and the skirting covers the joint. This gives the best thermal continuity and is the right approach if doing both at once.
What you need to measure
Total length of external-wall perimeter at ground floor level. Include all external walls; exclude party walls unless the house is a detached property.
Depth available behind the skirting board. A Victorian or Edwardian skirting is typically 100-150 mm tall and 18-25 mm thick. It conceals at least 100 mm of wall base.
Whether the floor slab is solid concrete (common post-1940s), raised concrete (beam-and-block), or suspended timber. Each has a different perimeter detail.
Worked example: 1930s semi, solid concrete floor, no existing wall insulation
External perimeter: 28 m. Floor: 75 mm concrete screed over existing slab, no insulation yet. Walls: uninsulated 280 mm cavity brick.
Step one: remove the skirting boards from all external walls. A bolster chisel behind the skirting releases it with minimal damage. Number each section on the back.
Step two: cut 25 mm PIR board into strips 150 mm wide, one length per room run. Bond to the base of the wall with dry-wall adhesive dabs. The top edge of the PIR strip should sit at screed level.
Step three: seal the junction between PIR and the concrete slab surface with low-expansion foam, then tape with aluminium foil tape.

Step four: refit the skirting boards over the PIR. The skirting covers the foil face. Pin to the PIR using adhesive rather than nails (nails through PIR into the wall create small thermal bridges at each pin).
Estimated annual saving: 420 kWh, roughly 25 pounds at 6p/kWh gas. Payback at 150 pounds material cost: 6 years. The wall assembly
U-value
improves further if the walls are subsequently insulated.
When to call a professional

This job is generally DIY-friendly. Call a professional if the floor slab shows rising damp (salt tide marks or wet patches in winter), if the external wall has a damp-proof course below floor level, or if the property is PAS 2035 funded and needs a registered assessor to sign off the work.
Done well vs done badly

Done well: the PIR strip runs the full length of every external wall, laps onto the floor surface by 50-100 mm, is sealed at every joint, and the skirting is refitted flush. On a cold morning, the base of the wall is noticeably warmer than before.
Done badly: strips applied to some walls only, gaps at corners, and skirting nailed through the PIR into the wall. Each nail creates a thermal short-circuit.

attrs.fix: Plane 2-3 mm off the back face of the skirting, or rebate a channel in the PIR face where the skirting sits.
attrs.cause: PIR board is thicker than the skirting depth, or the skirting was glued with a thick bead rather than fixed back
attrs.symptom: Skirting sits proud of the wall after refitting over PIR

attrs.fix: Remove the insulation strip, investigate the DPC continuity, and address the moisture source before re-insulating.
attrs.cause: Rising damp was already present but hidden by warm wall surface; insulation has dropped surface temperature below dew point at a wetter section of the wall
attrs.symptom: Damp appears at the base of the wall after insulation

initialFindings.message: paragraph reads at grade 11.2 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
initialFindings.snippet: Both floor and wall insulation planned: sequence the work so the floor insulation is installed first, the wall insulation runs down and laps
